title: First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for III.A.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that TSA SD III.A (Cybersecurity plan) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.16.29: First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.16.29' running on pan:traffic, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, nozomi:alert, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: `pan:traffic`, SCADA/HMI audit, PAM, `nozomi:alert`, NetFlow, ITSM, passive OT asset discovery
app: Splunk OT Security Add-on (5151), Splunk Enterprise Security (263), Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks (2757), Splunk Industrial Asset Intelligence, Splunk Add-on for CyberArk (4295), Splunk Edge Hub, Splunk Common Information Model Add-on (1621). (Splunkbase)
spl:
index=os sourcetype=stash:file_integrity path="*OT_Patch_Repo*" earliest=-90d
| eval uc_tag="22.16.29"
| stats earliest(_time) as first_seen by sha256, path
| eventstats earliest(first_seen) as g by sha256
| where first_seen=g AND first_seen > relative_time(now(),"-30d@d")
description: Supports pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes".
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for TSA SD compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes os and sourcetypes stash:file_integrity. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope; (2) Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates; (3) Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality; (4) Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields; (5) Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Indexes required: `index=os`.
visualization: Time chart, Choropleth or site bar, Table, Single value. Key fields: `first_seen`, `path`, `sha256`, `uc_tag`.

references.title: Splunk Add-on for Palo Alto Networks

knownFalsePositives:
1. **Authorised compliance operations** — The compliance and governance team performs routine control testing, evidence gathering, and policy reviews that generate legitimate activity matching this use case's detection patterns. These operations are documented in the compliance programme calendar and should be excluded by the compliance-ops service account tag.

2. **Scheduled system maintenance windows** — Approved maintenance activities including patching, upgrades, and configuration changes produce expected control deviations during documented change windows that are pre-approved through the change advisory board (CAB).

3. **External audit and assessment activities** — During periodic audits (internal or external), assessors perform sampling, testing, and evidence collection activities that generate unusual access patterns covered by the engagement letter and audit notification.

4. **Third-party service provider operations** — Contracted service providers performing legitimate operations under signed agreements generate expected activity that may trigger monitoring rules during normal service delivery windows.

**Suppression mechanism:** Maintain a `compliance_exception_register` KV Store lookup containing approved maintenance windows, audit engagement periods, service provider identifiers, and compliance team service accounts. The correlation search joins this lookup and suppresses matches where `exception_status=approved` and the exception remains within its validity period.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Indexes**: `os` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Data Model Acceleration**: The CIM datamodel referenced in the search must be accelerated. Validate with `| rest /services/admin/summarization | search summary.id=*` and confirm `summary.complete=100`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `III.A`. The control objective is: "Evidence that TSA SD III.A (Cybersecurity plan) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.16.29: First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is bespoke for this UC — no single canonical TA parses every source the detection relies on. Route data into `os` under `stash:file_integrity`, and document the ingestion path in the UC runbook so future engineers know where each field originates.


5 operational considerations apply to the rollout of this detection:


• **Hint 1:** Confirm field extractions and CIM tags for the sourcetypes in scope. CIM-compliance tagging must be validated end-to-end: a downstream datamodel that does not accelerate because of a missing tag is a silent failure.

• **Hint 2:** Load or maintain the referenced lookups/KVStore collections with owner attestation dates. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 3:** Schedule the search at an interval aligned to the control’s materiality. Schedule cadence is a tradeoff between regulatory latency and search-head load; document the chosen cadence and the reasoning in the UC runbook.

• **Hint 4:** Route positive findings to the compliance ticketing queue with required evidence fields. Routing targets (SOC, compliance team, specific queue) must be documented with named on-call owners; an 'alerts go to the team' runbook fails at 3am on Sunday.

• **Hint 5:** Retain scheduled search exports per records management and legal hold procedures. Report-generation cadence must match the regulatory reporting cycle; a monthly digest satisfies an annual review requirement but not a quarterly one.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

`eventstats` attaches a group-level aggregate to every row in the result set without collapsing rows. For "First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes" this gives each event per-peer context — a row can be compared to its group's median, average, or quantiles without losing the row-level fields the detection relies on.

For "First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes," the aggregation produces the result shape required by the TSA SD control — each row is a summary record the regulation expects to be presentable at audit time. The SPL tags each event with `uc_tag="22.16.29"` — these constants are the detection's identity signature; any downstream search ('alert all of one detection family,' 'evidence for clause X') can filter on these constants instead of re-deriving from the full base search.

The grouping `by sha256, path` is the analytical unit — the aggregate is computed within each distinct combination. The primary dimension here is `sha256` — the detection's finding is effectively 'a sha256 whose behaviour diverges from its own historical baseline or its peer group,' not a population-wide threshold. A common mistake is grouping by a field that has cardinality near 1:1 with events, which produces a group-per-event and makes the window stat trivially equal to the event value.

Memory sensitivity: `eventstats` must hold the entire result set in memory to compute per-row aggregates. For searches returning >1M events, this can OOM the search head. If you see `fatal=true` in `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd` or mrsparkle restarts, reduce the base-search time window or pre-aggregate before `eventstats`.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.16.29: First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.16.29,reg=TSA_SD
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=os sourcetype=stash:file_integrity earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `first_seen`, `path`, `sha256`, `uc_tag`. Run:

```spl
index=os sourcetype=stash:file_integrity earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table first_seen, path, sha256, uc_tag
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.16.29: TSA SD evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Time trend (the key fields):** Place in the main content row. Use `timechart span=1d` aggregated appropriately for the regulation's review cadence (daily for PCI/HIPAA, weekly for ISO-style controls). Annotate the chart with control-change dates so auditors can see when mitigations took effect.

**Panel 2 — Choropleth Or Site Bar (the key fields):** Configure according to standard Splunk visualization patterns for this panel type.

**Panel 3 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 4 — Single-value KPI (the key fields):** Place at top-left. Configure `underLabel` with the regulation clause (e.g. 'TSA SD current violation count') and set a red colour range for any non-zero value. A zero reading is the steady state for a healthy control.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**Search returns no results when violations are expected:** Verify time-window alignment, index existence, and field extractions in that order. Most 'missing results' are simpler configuration issues before they're detection-logic issues.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**TSA SD-specific failure modes:**

• Covered surface transportation: TSA SDs cover specific rail, pipeline, and transit operators. Detections must scope to covered operations; non-covered assets are noise.

• Cybersecurity Coordinator designation: each covered entity has a designated CC. Detections on IR must route through the CC's communication channel; bypassing the CC is a procedural finding.

• CISA reporting timelines: different incident types have different reporting windows (e.g. 24h for certain classes). Detections on reporting SLA must encode the specific window per incident type.

• OT/IT segmentation: TSA SDs strongly emphasise OT/IT separation. Detections on cross-domain access must identify which side is which; an alert that doesn't know its physical/cyber domain is ambiguous.
grandmaExplanation: We support pipeline cybersecurity program oversight under TSA Pipeline Security Directive by correlating operational telemetry with governance records for "First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes". That lines up with pipeline security expectations and helps the team show its work to regulators and leadership.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Inject a synthetic test event that satisfies the detection criteria for 'First-seen OT patch-repository binary hashes' — for example, create a log entry with all required fields populated to match the SPL filter conditions. Verify the search fires within the expected schedule interval, the alert populates the compliance dashboard, and the event routes correctly to the evidence index with proper TSA SD control tagging.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Ingest a batch of test events that are structurally similar but explicitly do NOT meet the detection threshold — e.g., events with compliant values, timestamps outside the monitoring window, or source systems excluded by the scope filter. Verify the search produces zero results, no false-positive alerts fire, and the compliance posture score remains unaffected for the TSA SD control objective.

evidence: Saved search results archived to the `audit_evidence` index with TSA SD control tagging; scheduled PDF/CSV exports for auditor consumption; Splunk dashboard screenshots with timestamp watermarks capturing control operating effectiveness over the review period.
exclusions: Excludes test/sandbox environments, pre-production systems, development instances without regulated data, and any systems formally documented as out-of-scope in the TSA SD programme scope statement approved by the control owner.
